author: The Didache (Teaching of the Twelve Apostles)
note: The Two Ways opening — the moral catechesis of the earliest church (c. AD 90–110), grounding all of Christian ethics in love of God and neighbour, matching Jesus' summary of the Law (Matt. 22:37–40). Cited for the ANTIQUITY and continuity of Christian moral teaching, not to settle any intra-Christian debate. SEED — WebFetch summarizer modernized/paraphrased archaic pronouns (thou/thee → you); wording reconstructed to the stable Roberts–Donaldson ANF text and now verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: There are two ways, one of life and one of death; but a great difference between the two ways. The way of life, then, is this: First, thou shalt love God who made thee; second, thy neighbour as thyself; and all things whatsoever thou wouldst should not occur to thee, thou also to another do not do.

author: The Didache (Teaching of the Twelve Apostles)
note: Cited for the ANTIQUITY of the triune baptismal formula — 'into the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit' (cf. Matt. 28:19) attested in the earliest church order, c. AD 90–110. Denominational neutrality: this documents that triune baptism is primitive; it does NOT adjudicate intra-Christian debates on the mode or efficacy of baptism (the text itself allows immersion in living water, other water, or triple pouring). SEED — summarizer modernized archaic pronouns; wording reconstructed to Roberts–Donaldson ANF and now verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: And concerning baptism, thus baptize ye: Having first said all these things, baptize into the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit, in living water. But if thou have not living water, baptize into other water; and if thou canst not in cold, in warm. But if thou have not either, pour out water thrice upon the head into the name of Father and Son and Holy Spirit.

author: The Didache (Teaching of the Twelve Apostles)
note: The earliest surviving eucharistic thanksgiving prayers (c. AD 90–110). The scattered-grain image — one loaf from many grains as a figure of the one Church gathered from the ends of the earth — is cited for the ANTIQUITY of eucharistic thanksgiving and the church-unity theme, NOT to adjudicate intra-Christian debates on the nature or efficacy of the Eucharist. NOTE: Roberts–Donaldson renders the grain image 'scattered over the hills'; some reproductions read 'scattered upon the mountains'; the Roberts–Donaldson 'over the hills' was confirmed at verification. SEED — summarizer modernized/paraphrased archaic pronouns; wording now verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: Now concerning the Eucharist, thus give thanks. First, concerning the cup: We thank thee, our Father, for the holy vine of David Thy servant, which Thou madest known to us through Jesus Thy Servant; to Thee be the glory for ever. And concerning the broken bread: We thank Thee, our Father, for the life and knowledge which Thou madest known to us through Jesus Thy Servant; to Thee be the glory for ever. Even as this broken bread was scattered over the hills, and was gathered together and became one, so let Thy Church be gathered together from the ends of the earth into Thy kingdom; for Thine is the glory and the power through Jesus Christ for ever.

author: The Didache (Teaching of the Twelve Apostles)
note: The thanksgiving after the meal, closing with the primitive Aramaic acclamation 'Maranatha' (cf. 1 Cor. 16:22) — evidence of very early, Semitic-rooted Christian worship. Cited for the ANTIQUITY of eucharistic thanksgiving and its eschatological hope; not an adjudication of intra-Christian sacramental debate. SEED — summarizer modernized/paraphrased archaic pronouns; wording now verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: But after ye are filled, thus give thanks: We thank Thee, holy Father, for Thy holy name which Thou didst cause to tabernacle in our hearts, and for the knowledge and faith and immortality, which Thou madest known to us through Jesus Thy Servant; to Thee be the glory for ever. Remember, Lord, Thy Church, to deliver it from all evil and to make it perfect in Thy love, and gather it from the four winds, sanctified for Thy kingdom which Thou hast prepared for it; for Thine is the power and the glory for ever. Let grace come, and let this world pass away. Hosanna to the God (Son) of David! If any one is holy, let him come; if any one is not so, let him repent. Maranatha. Amen.

author: The Didache (Teaching of the Twelve Apostles)
note: The Lord's Day gathering to break bread and give thanks, preceded by confession and reconciliation (c. AD 90–110). Cited for the ANTIQUITY of weekly Lord's-Day Christian assembly and eucharistic worship; not an adjudication of intra-Christian debate on the sabbath/Lord's-Day question or eucharistic theology. NOTE: modern translations (Lake/Holmes) read 'on the Lord's own day'; Roberts–Donaldson reads 'every Lord's day' — the public-domain RD wording is used here deliberately. SEED — wording now verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: But every Lord's day gather yourselves together, and break bread, and give thanksgiving after having confessed your transgressions, that your sacrifice may be pure. But let no one that is at variance with his fellow come together with you, until they be reconciled, that your sacrifice may not be profaned.

author: The Didache (Teaching of the Twelve Apostles)
note: The eschatological close — a call to watchfulness for the Lord's coming (cf. Matt. 24–25), c. AD 90–110, showing the earliest church's living expectation of Christ's return. Cited for the ANTIQUITY of Christian eschatological hope; it does NOT take a position on any intra-Christian end-times scheme (guardrail: end-times is a second-order debate). NOTE: Roberts–Donaldson reads 'our Lord cometh'; the WebFetch summarizer rendered it 'will come' — archaic wording reconstructed and now verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: Watch for your life's sake. Let not your lamps be quenched, nor your loins unloosed; but be ready, for ye know not the hour in which our Lord cometh. But come together often, seeking the things which are befitting to your souls: for the whole time of your faith will not profit you, if ye be not made perfect in the last time.
